schools.summary: Ambrose: the good Physician shows a remedy after the fall, the merciful Judge offers pardon; Theophylact: Christ took flesh to receive sinners as the physician the sick; the Gloss: publicans the despised tax-farmers — the outcast drawn to Christ.

Barnes: the Pharisees judged Christ by his company, blind that he received sinners to heal not share their sin; Clarke: 'receiveth sinners' (takes to his bosom) is the gospel itself — the reproach turned to highest praise.

Robertson: Christ answers the cavillers with parables, championing the lost (Ragg's climax: Pasture/House/Home, Herdsman/Housewife/Father, Sheep/Treasure/Son); Wesley: three parables of one import — how God receives sinners; Gill: to convict, encourage, and vindicate.

Clarke: the flock all mankind, the Divine Shepherd Christ; the sheep an emblem of the heedless straying sinner, helpless to return; the shepherd goes 'until he find it' — the persevering pursuit of grace; Lightfoot on the Jewish 'ninety-nine.'

themes: go after that which is lost

schools.summary: Gill: Christ finds the sheep helpless and dead in sin, gives life, rescues from Satan, cleanses, clothes with his righteousness, and bears it home; Ellicott on the sheep laid on the shoulders — grace carrying the helpless sinner, the whole salvation the Shepherd's work, done rejoicing.

Gill: the 'home' is the church and heaven, whither grace alone carries the found; Ellicott on the 'friends and neighbours' (cf. Luke 14:12) summoned to share — the shepherd's joy overflowing and communal, a rebuke to the Pharisees' grudging.
Gill/Lightfoot: joy in heaven (and the church) over the penitent; 'just persons' = the truly righteous in Jewish usage, occasioning no such recovery.

Augustine: the ninety-nine read ironically of the self-righteous who 'need no repentance' only in their own conceit — rebuking the murmuring Pharisees; the joy greater because the recovery is the more unexpected mercy.

debates.question: Who are the 'ninety-nine just persons which need no repentance'?

sides.position: The truly righteous, occasioning no dramatic recovery
Ironically, the self-righteous who only think they need none

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the coin bears the King's image — man made in God's likeness, precious though lost; Gregory the Great: the woman is God/the divine Wisdom, the ten = nine angelic orders + man the tenth; Augustine: the nine = the self-trusting; the diligent search (candle, sweeping) for the precious thing.

outliers.note: Reads the ten coins as the nine orders of angels and man created as the tenth, to fill up the number of the elect.

themes: seek diligently till she find it
the worth of the lost soul

schools.summary: Robertson: her female friends (feminine forms); 'which I had lost' — the woman owns the loss (grace on the seeker's side); the call 'rejoice with me' echoes the shepherd, pressing the same communal joy over the recovered.

themes: found the piece I had lost

schools.summary: Robertson: 'in the presence of the angels' = the joy of God himself before his court; Barnes: God has no pleasure in the wicked's death but joy in his turning (Ezekiel 33:11).
Calvin: the greater joy not because the righteous are less prized, but because the recovery of one devoted to destruction displays God's mercy more brightly — joy from an unexpected good.

themes: joy in the presence of the angels

schools.summary: Ambrose: the three parables a threefold remedy — Shepherd's pity (sheep), Church's seeking (coin), Father's reconciliation (son); the prodigal the deepest, the sinner a beloved child who willfully departs and must return by repentance; the two sons = penitent sinners and murmuring Pharisees.

themes: a certain man had two sons

schools.summary: Gill: the younger son the elect among sinners in a state of nature — conceited, impatient of restraint, withdrawing from God's yoke; the demand for his portion = the sinner wanting God's gifts but not his rule; God divides his living, permitting the willful their course.
Ambrose: no fault in the father's giving, for no age is weak in the kingdom and God's gifts are freely bestowed.

Chrysostom: the 'far country' not a place but the heart's departure — the sinner flees God in disposition, to stand afar off and sin freely; Gill: the carnal man living independent, wasting God's gifts on his lusts; the swift descent from independence to riotous waste.

themes: the swift waste of God's gifts

schools.summary: Gill: sin strips a man of every good (the image of God, righteousness, strength), leaving him poor, blind, naked — so there is no free will or native goodness in fallen man, all good coming from grace; the famine exposing the want, the world unable to satisfy the soul.

themes: the emptiness of the far country

schools.summary: Gill: the 'citizen' read as an unregenerate, legal preacher to whom the awakened sinner first turns (gifts without grace, a form without power); the swine-feeding the lowest degradation, sin's freedom become harsh bondage.
Barnes: he hired himself to the lowest service; for a Jew, feeding swine the depth of degradation — the soul enslaved to its basest lusts.

debates.question: Who is the 'citizen of that country'?

sides.position: An unregenerate, legal preacher the awakened sinner first turns to
Simply a master of that godless land — the degrading bondage of sin

schools.summary: Gill: the husks (carob, swine's food) = the world's pleasures, riches, lusts — mean, empty, unsatisfying food that cannot fill the soul; 'no man gave unto him' — the far country abandons the ruined sinner, the world a hard master giving nothing in need.

themes: the husks the swine did eat

schools.summary: Ambrose: 'came to himself' — in departing from the Father he departed from himself; returning to God restores the soul to itself; Augustine: he brought his mind back from outward enticements to the conscience; Gregory of Nyssa: chastened by hardship; the Father's bread-to-spare piercing him.

debates.note: Reads 'came to himself' as the soul's recovery of its true identity — sin a departure from oneself, repentance a return.

themes: hired servants have bread to spare

schools.summary: Barnes: 'I will arise' a decisive resolve to return at once; the sinner has nowhere to go but to God, trusting the kindness he wronged; Gill: he forsakes the far country itself; the confession owns sin against God first ('heaven') then the father — self-accusation, the heart of repentance.

themes: I will arise and go to my father

schools.summary: Gill: he owns his unworthiness to be called a son in any sense; Barnes: the request shows deep humility (a sinner's), love for the Father's house, and confidence in his kindness — content with the servant's place, casting himself wholly on mercy, though grace will give far more than he asks.

themes: no more worthy to be called thy son

schools.summary: Gill: the son's arising and coming carried out by grace, not his own strength.

Barnes: the father saw him 'a great way off' (watching for him), ran (where the son came slowly), embraced and kissed him before his confession — the eager, anticipating love of God meeting the penitent while yet far off.

themes: his father saw him and ran
fell on his neck and kissed him

schools.summary: Clarke: the son's plea 'make me a servant' is cut short (the added words a later harmonizing gloss) — the father's love cannot wait for the full confession but interrupts with pardoning assurance; grace gives more than the penitent asks, restoring him as a son not a servant.

debates.question: Why does the son not ask to be made a hired servant here?

sides.position: The father's love interrupts before he can; the added words are a later gloss, and the omission is the parable's beauty

schools.summary: Gill: the eloquent 'but' of grace — the father hears nothing of the servant's place; the best robe figuring Christ's righteousness covering the sinner's rags.

Barnes: the best robe (festal, Genesis 27:15), the ring (dignity and adoption, Genesis 41:42), the shoes (the free son, not the barefoot slave) — the full and free restoration to sonship, the sinner justified, adopted, adorned.

Gill: the fatted calf figures Christ — either the sacrifice slain in the sinner's stead (atonement) or the rich spiritual food the believer feeds on; Barnes: 'be merry' = 'eating, let us rejoice' — the choicest of the herd for the greatest joy, the feast centred on Christ.

themes: let us eat and be merry

schools.summary: Gill: the son 'dead' = dead in Adam, in law, in trespasses (separation from God, his image defaced); conversion a passage from death to life, lostness to recovery — the greatest change, the fittest occasion of the greatest joy; the father owns him 'my son.'

themes: was dead and is alive again

schools.summary: Bede: the third parable not only declares the joy but blames the envious (the elder son); Augustine: the elder son = Israel in the field of the law's earthly labours, not in the house; the music and dancing = the Spirit-filled gospel joy of the Church — the murmuring Pharisees figured in him.

themes: his elder son was in the field
the second movement of the parable

schools.summary: Robertson: the servant a serving-boy (not the bondslave of v.22); the elder son 'inquired' eagerly and repeatedly, keeping outside; Gill: he questions a servant (a minister/disciple) as the Pharisees did when offended — near the house but not in it, inquiring about grace from afar.

themes: he called one of the servants
near the house but not in it

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'safe and sound' (in health) Luke the physician's word (cf. 5:31; 7:10) — recovery of body and soul; Robertson: 'hath gotten him back'; the servant names him 'thy brother' (the kinship the elder son will deny); the father's joy at receiving him back, not reward for merit.

Gill: the anger is self-righteousness' hatred of free grace — displeased that salvation is free, full, for sinners, by Christ alone; Clarke: the indignation of the scribes and Pharisees (Luke 15:1-2); the father comes out and entreats — grace stooping to the resentful as to the sinner.

themes: he was angry, would not go in

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'I serve, as a slave serves' — servile obedience from fear and hope of reward, not love; Clarke: the complaint unjust (the father had divided his living, the son wanted nothing); Barnes: grudging even a kid — the mercenary, loveless self-righteousness of the Pharisee, measuring love by reward.

themes: these many years do I serve thee

schools.summary: Barnes: 'this thy son' (not 'my brother') a phrase of studied contempt, disowning the kinship and reproaching the father; casting up the worst sins to make the welcome seem unjust; Gill: the true offence is free grace to sinners, that the worst should be received so freely and so soon — the murmuring Pharisee mirrored.

Barnes: the elder son lost nothing (the property divided, all the remainder his); the father reasons gently, owning him 'Son'; the parable's design — the elder son the Pharisees, the younger the returning sinner, the father God; those who abide with God lack nothing and have no cause to grudge grace to others.

themes: Son, thou art ever with me
all that I have is thine

schools.summary: Gill: the joy was 'meet' for all, the elder brother too, had he the spiritual affection to rejoice at sinners' conversion; Clarke: the father gently turns the elder's 'this thy son' into 'this thy brother,' restoring the kinship — the parable ends open, the appeal left hanging before the Pharisees and every reader.

themes: it was meet to make merry
this thy brother was dead and is alive
